Populates the <synphot_dir>/crds file cache with all synphot files
implied by self.args.context.   This creates an ordinary CRDS cache

and can optimize future downloads if desired.

Construct the mapping from synphot file basename to classic CDBS path for
every file defined by the TMC and TMT tables.  Also include mtab paths
for the master TMC,TMT,TMG tables themselves.

This bridges the gap between basenames recorded by CRDS and synphot
paths which are defined with environment variables encoded in the table
filenames.

Reads table files ending with _tmt.fits and _tmc.fits

Returns   { syn_basename : full_pysn_path, ... }

embedded iraf$-style path env var into a normal filepath.  This is used
to locate files within appropriate sub-directories of
<synphot_dir>/comp.

Return the mapping from a component file basename as defined in the CRDS
rmap to the absolute path in a CDBS-style synphot file repo being created.

Returns  { component_basename : abs_pysyn_path, ...}

Returns   { table_basename(tab) : pysyn_mtab_filepath }

On OS-X and Linux this creates files which are referenced from two
different paths on the file system.   Deleting one path or the other
leaves behind a normal file referenced from only one location.

returns   None

minimum data level associated with reference type `reftype`.  Nominally
this means fetching and interpreting the SYSTEM DATALVL reference file so
utilizing this function requires operating in an environment with an
up-to-date and accessible CRDS cache.

and return the type_to_level mapping implied by the cal_levels field.

functions.name: crds.misc.datalvl.invert_cal_levels
documentation:
Invert and return the cal_levels mapping.

```pycon
>>> cal_levels = {
...    "2A": [
...        "dark",
...        "gain",
...        "ipc",
...    ],
...    "2B": [
...        "area",
...        "camera",
...        "collimator",
...    ],
...    "3": [
...        "distortion",
...        "drizpars",
...    ]
... }
```

```pycon
>>> inverted = invert_cal_levels(cal_levels)
>>> sorted(inverted.items())
[('area', '2B'), ('camera', '2B'), ('collimator', '2B'), ('dark', '2A'), ('distortion', '3'), ('drizpars', '3'), ('gain', '2A'), ('ipc', '2A')]
```

```pycon
>>> tricky_levels = {
...    "1A" : [ "distortion"],
...    "2A" : [ "distortion"],
...    }
>>> sorted(invert_cal_levels(tricky_levels).items())
[('distortion', '1A')]
```

modules.name: crds.core.pysh
documentation:
pysh is syntactic sugar which simplifies using the Python
subprocess module for writing command line utility scripts that one
might ordinarily write in a UNIX shell, .e.g. bash, ksh, or csh.

While the subprocess module provides a flexible basis for launching
other programs in a wide variety of contexts, it does lack the
simplicity of shell script for basic tasks.  Pysh is simple sugar
intended to glue a limited subset of shell script into Python.

Taking from the shellscript example of the subprocess module
implementation illustrates what pysh is really about:

First,  shellscript might look like this:

   output=`dmesg | grep hda`

This shellscript can be translated into Python subprocess module code
like this:

   p1 = Popen(["dmesg"], stdout=PIPE)
   p2 = Popen(["grep", "hda"], stdin=p1.stdout, stdout=PIPE)
   output = p2.communicate()[0]

But using pysh,  built upon the subprocess module,  one would say this:

   output = out("dmesg | grep hda")

Pysh supports a syntax very similar to UNIX shell substitution which
is interpreted in the scope the pysh subprocess is called from, e.g.

    filename1 = "something.fits"
    out("ls -l ${filename1}")

is functionally equivalent to:

    out("ls -l 'something.fits'")

Note that each substitution value is limited to being a single UNIX word.
! /usr/bin/env python

functions.name: crds.core.pysh.set_debug
documentation:
Set the global debug status for pysh, affecting Shell debug default
settings.  Nominally set to True in test/debug environments.

Since setting debug mode changes the default behavior of Shell, it should
only be used temporarily to ensure

documentation:

a namespace dictionary combining his globals and locals.  `backup` is the
number of stack frames to back up relative to the caller of _context().
handle peeking back up the call tree into the caller's namespace so that
caller variable names can be substituted using ${} notation.  Also handle
command line parameters using $* and $1, etc.

functions.name: crds.core.pysh.lines

Convenience routines not related to subprocesses here.

functions.name: crds.core.pysh.fail
documentation:
Quit the program,  issuing a message which is the join of *args.
Use keyword `status` as the program exit code or -1 if unspecified.

functions.name: crds.core.pysh.usage
documentation:
Emit a standard program usage message based on the min and max
command line parameter counts.   If the program takes at least
one parameter,  min_args should be 1.   If the program takes at most
one parameter, max_args should also be 1.

functions.name: crds.core.pysh.arg
documentation:
Ultra-simple convenience function for extracting command line parameters by
index, optionally using a `default` value if fewer than `index` parameters
are provided.  The function `typecast` can be set to a python type object
to cast parameter strings to other python types like int or float.

$ program  arg1 arg2 arg3

progname = arg(0)
arg1 = arg(1, "foo")
arg2 = arg(2, 57, int)
arg3 = arg(3, 45.0, float)

functions.name: crds.core.pysh.__rewrite_shell_statement

Return the substitution for re-writing a line of pysh-script as
Python.
Code for rewriting a file of pysh-script as an ordinary python module.

Execute the commands in this Shell expression and either raise

Shell performs sh-like variable substitutions and returns a callable
object which runs `command` as a subprocess when called.   The shell object
records the final command line and manages program I/O,  capturing output
and error status as attributes which can be inspected later.

modules.name: crds.core.crds_cache_locking
documentation:
This module defines locking primitives for the CRDS cache used to prevent
simultaneous writes.  This is principally motivated by the JWST association
logic which may attempt to prefetch files for multiple images at the same time,
often in a multiprocessing expansion running multiple associations concurrently.

CRDS locking wraps more primitive locking functionality such as that provided by
the lockfile package or the multiprocessing module.  In general locks should be
assumed to be non-recursive and will deadlock if the owner attempts to acquire a
second instance.

A number of configuration env var settings control locking behavior, see
crds.core.config for more info.

documentation: Based on CRDS configuration,  return the lock class.

Return a lock context manager to guard the CRDS cache against concurrent
writes.

documentation: Clear all CRDS cache file locks.

functions.name: crds.core.crds_cache_locking.init_lock
documentation: Create lock `lockname` without guarding global LOCKS.  Warn on fail.
To avoid a race condition creating a multiprocessing lock,  at a minimum

documentation:
There was a problem pushing or retrieving the value of a pipeline context
recorded on the CRDS server, i.e. the pipeline's echo of the context
they're using as reported by the sync tool.   A remote context is CRDS's
attempt to track the default context actually sync'ed to a pipeline, i.e.
what that particular cache says.  In contrast the operational context
is a global maintained on the CRDS server defining what the default *should
be* as a result of successful and current cache sync'ing by any pipeline
or CRDS user.

them.   NOTE: allowing ambiguous matches is important to HST and the
canonical HST Match -> UseAfter pattern can support them under many
circumstances.  The semantics for these are shaky since it's possible
to have merge collisions which work out badly.

This module defines dictionary-like clasess of various forms nominally used to support
CRDS mappings:

TransformedDict --   generic MutableMapping with key and value transforms
    Derived from discussion at http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3387691/python-how-to-perfectly-override-a-dict

LazyFileDict   --  Demand loaded network of files (aka CRDS context)

class_variables.name: crds.core.custom_dict.LazyFileDict.special_values_set
documentation: Override in sub-classes as needed

Instantiates a graph of inter-referencing files (like CRDS Mappings) on
 demand basis as particular paths are accessed.

Manages selections for higher level mappings like .pmaps and .imaps which
refer to other mapping files.  Loads submappings on a demand basis rather
than exhaustively at startup.  This is motivated by a need to minimize file
operations in web/cloud applications and highly concurrent environments
like calibration pipelines.  It opens the potential for only loading a
single instrument under a given context.

Values included in special_values_set are excluded from nested loading
and kept as literals,  e.g. N/A

modules.name: crds.core.reftypes
documentation:
This module consolidates a number of aspects of reference type definition which were
originally reverse engineered from CDBS and several different spec files.  It is
organized around loading type specs or prototype rmaps from the "specs" subdirectory of
an observatory/subsystem package.   For HST this reduces defining new types to adding
a prototype rmap and defining .tpn files in the observatory package.

functions.name: crds.core.reftypes._invert_instr_dict
documentation:
Invert a set of nested dictionaries of the form {instr: {key: val}}
to create a dict of the form {instr: {val: key}}.
Global table loads used at operational runtime:

functions.name: crds.core.reftypes.load_specs
documentation:
Load either the combined .json formatted type specs (preferred) or specs from
individual type spec files (if necessary).

functions.name: crds.core.reftypes.load_raw_specs
documentation:
Return a dictionary of TypeSpecs loaded from directory `spec_path` of form:

{ instrument : { filetype : header_dict, ...}, ... }

functions.name: crds.core.reftypes.save_json_specs
documentation: Write out the specs dictionary returned by _load_specs() as .json in one combined file.

functions.name: crds.core.reftypes.load_json_specs
documentation: Load specs from the .json combined spec file under `specs_path`.

functions.name: crds.core.reftypes.from_package_file
documentation:
Given the __file__ from a package,  load specs from the package's specs subdirectory
and construct and return a TypeParameters object.

functions.name: crds.core.reftypes.get_types_object
documentation:
Each observatory package instantiates a types object from the observatory's
type specs using generic code defined in this module;  any generic code related
to types is defined here,  but ultimately the instantiate TYPES object is bound
to a specific observatory and defined in that package.

classes.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeSpec

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeSpec.__init__
documentation:
Initialize this TypeSpec from dict `header`,  enforcing requirments and creating suitable
defaults for missing fields.

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeSpec.from_file
documentation:
For historical HST types,  build type info from a spec file derived from CDBS specs like
reference_file_defs.xml or cdbscatalog.dat.  For new CRDS-only types,  use a prototype rmap
with an enhanced header to define the type.   Prototypes should be submissible but should not
contain references.
This class captures type definition parameters for a single type

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeParameters.reference_props_to_validator_keys
documentation:
Return the sequence of validator keys associated with `filename`.   A validator key
is nominally a .tpn filename and can vary by observatory, instrument, and type as well

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeParameters.reference_name_to_tpninfos
documentation:
Return the .tpn file key associated with reference `filename`.
Strictly speaking this should be driven by mapping_validator_key...  but the interface
for that is wrong so slave it to reference_name_to_tpn_key instead,  historically
one-for-one.

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeParameters.reference_name_to_tpn_text
documentation: Given reference `filename`,  return the text of the corresponding .tpn

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeParameters.reference_name_to_ld_tpn_text
documentation: Given reference `filename`,  return the text of the corresponding _ld.tpn

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeParameters.get_row_keys
documentation:
Return the row_keys which define unique table rows corresponding to mapping.

These are used for "mode" checks to issue warnings when unique rows are deleted
in a certify comparison check against the preceding version of a table.

row_keys are now also utlized to perform "affected datasets" table row
lookups which essentially requires emulating that aspect of the calibration
software.  Consequently, row_keys now have a requirement for a higher level
of fidelity since they were originally defined for mode checks, since the
consequences of inadequate row keys becomes failed "affects checks" and not
merely an extraneous warning.  In their capacity as affected datasets
parameters,  row_keys must be supported by the interface which connects the
CRDS server to the appropriate system dataset parameter database,  DADSOPS
for HST.   That interface must be updated when row_keys.dat is changed.

The certify mode checks have a shaky foundation since the concept of mode
doesn't apply to all tables and sometimes "data" parameters are required to
render rows unique.   The checks only issue warnings however so they can be
ignored by file submitters.

For HST calibration references mapping is an rmap.

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeParameters.get_row_keys_by_instrument
documentation:
To support defining the CRDS server interface to DADSOPS, return the
sorted list of row keys necessary to perform all the table lookups
of an instrument.   These (FITS) keywords will be used to instrospect
DADSOPS and identify table fields which provide the necessary parameters.

methods.name: crds.core.reftypes.TypeParameters.get_filekinds

and type field inter-relationships and cache them as attributes.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.flatten
documentation:
Given a sequence possibly containing nested lists or tuples,
flatten the sequence to a single non-nested list of primitives.

```pycon
>>> flatten((('META.INSTRUMENT.DETECTOR', 'META.SUBARRAY.NAME'), ('META.OBSERVATION.DATE', 'META.OBSERVATION.TIME')))
['META.INSTRUMENT.DETECTOR', 'META.SUBARRAY.NAME', 'META.OBSERVATION.DATE', 'META.OBSERVATION.TIME']
```

functions.name: crds.core.utils.traced
documentation: Issue a verbose message showing parameters and possibly return val.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.gc_collected
documentation:
Run Python's gc.collect() before and after the decorated function.

This is pretty slow and may be overkill but was motivated by file
submission use cases such as "certify" and "insert_references" which
iterate over large numbers of reference files and,  particularly when
examining arrays,  may easily exhaust memory,  sometimes leading to
silent OS or shell level crashes with no traceback.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.cached
documentation:
The cached decorator embeds a dictionary in a function wrapper to
capture prior results.

The wrapped function works like the original, except it's faster because it
fetches results for prior calls from the cache. The wrapped function has
extra attributes:

.cache -- { key(parameters): old_result } dictionary
.uncached(args, keys) -- original unwrapped function
.readonly(args, keys) -- function variant which uses but doesn't update cache
.cache_key(args, keys) -- returns tuple used to locate a function call result

```pycon
>>> @cached
... def sum(x,y):
...   print("really doing it.")
...   return x+y
```

The first call should actually call the unwrapped sum():

```pycon
>>> sum(1,2)
really doing it.
3
```

The second call will return the prior result found in the cache:

```pycon
>>> sum(1,2)
3
```

Dump or operate on the cache like this, it's just a dict:

```pycon
>>> sum.cache
{(1, 2): 3}
```

By-pass the cache and call the undecorated function like this:

```pycon
>>> sum.uncached(1,2)
really doing it.
3
```

Clear the cache like this:

```pycon
>>> sum.cache.clear()
>>> sum(1,2)
really doing it.
3
```

A variant of the function which reads but does not update the cache is available.
After calling the read_only variant the cache is not updated:

```pycon
>>> sum.cache.clear()
>>> sum.readonly(1,2)
really doing it.
3
>>> sum(1,2)
really doing it.
3
```

However, the readonly variant will exploit any values in the cache already:

```pycon
>>> sum(1,2)
3
```

functions.name: crds.core.utils.list_cached_functions
documentation: List all the functions supporting caching under @utils.cached or @utils.xcached.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.capture_output
documentation:
Decorate a function with @capture_output to define a CapturedFunction()
wrapper around it.

Doesn't currently capture non-python output but could with dup2.

Decorate any function to wrap it in a CapturedFunction() wrapper:

```pycon
>>> @capture_output
... def f(x,y):
...    print("hi")
...    return x + y
```

```pycon
>>> f
CapturedFunction('f')
```

Calling a captured function suppresses its output:

```pycon
>>> f(1, 2)
3
```

To call the original undecorated function:

```pycon
>>> f.uncaptured(1, 2)
hi
3
```

If you don't care about the return value, but want the output:

```pycon
>>> f.outputs(1, 2) == 'hi\n'
True
```

If you need both the return value and captured output:

```pycon
>>> f.returns_outputs(1, 2) == (3, 'hi\n')
True
```

is assumed to include a filename which will not be created.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.is_writable
documentation:
Interpret the mode bits of `filepath` in terms of the current user and it's groups,
returning True if any of user, group, or other have write permission on the path.

If `filepath` doesn't exist,  return `no_exist` if the directory is writable.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.copytree
documentation:
Derived from shutil.copytree() example with added function hooks called
on a per-directory, per-file, and per-symlink basis with (src, dest)
parameters.  Removes exception trapping since partial copies are useless
for CRDS.  Cannot handle devices or sockets, only regular files and
directories.   File stats not preserved.

Returns  contents of `s3_uri`.

or filename uri.   Reads to memory, intended for small files.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.checksum
documentation:
Return the CRDS hexdigest for file at `pathname`.   See also
copy_and_checksum() below which must match sha1sum results.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.copy_and_checksum
documentation:
Copy file from `source` path to `destination` path computing
sha1sum of source during the copy.   This is a *gross* server-side
optimization to prevent copying 10's of G's of data and then reading
it back just to compute sha1sum.   This is necessitated because it's
not possible to take ownership of files you can both read and delete
but it is possible to make a personally owned copy which replaces the
original.  See also checksum() which must have matching results.

functions.name: crds.core.utils.str_checksum
documentation:
Return the CRDS hexdigest for small strings. Likewise, must
match checksum() and copy_and_checksum() above.

```pycon
>>> str_checksum("this is a test.")
'7728f8eb7bf75ec3cc49364861eec852fc814870'
```

functions.name: crds.core.utils.condition_value
documentation:
Condition `value`, ostensibly taken from a FITS header or CDBS
reference file table, such that it is suitable for appearing in or
matching an rmap MatchSelector key.

```pycon
>>> condition_value('ANY')
'*'
```

\# >>> condition_value('-999')
'N/A'
\# >>> condition_value('-999.0')
'N/A'
\# >> condition_value('4294966297.0') # -999
'N/A'

```pycon
>>> condition_value('N/A')
'N/A'
>>> condition_value('NOT APPLICABLE')
'N/A'
>>> condition_value('NOT_APPLICABLE')
'N/A'
```

\>> condition_value('')
'N/A'

```pycon
>>> condition_value('4294967295')
'-1.0'
```

```pycon
>>> condition_value(False)
'F'
>>> condition_value(True)
'T'
```

```pycon
>>> condition_value(1)
'1.0'
>>> condition_value('-9')
'-9.0'
>>> condition_value('1.0')
'1.0'
>>> condition_value('1.')
'1.0'
```

```pycon
>>> condition_value('foo')
'FOO'
```

```pycon
>>> condition_value('iref$uaf12559i_drk.fits')
'IREF$UAF12559I_DRK.FITS'
```

```pycon
>>> condition_value('2013-11-05 15:21:34')
'2013-11-05 15:21:34'
```

functions.name: crds.core.utils.condition_header_keys
documentation:
Convert a matching parameter header into the form which supports eval(), ie.
JWST-style header keys. Nominally for JWST data model style keys.

```pycon
>>> from pprint import pprint as pp
>>> pp(condition_header_keys({"META.INSTRUMENT.NAME": "NIRISS"}))
{'META.INSTRUMENT.NAME': 'NIRISS', 'META_INSTRUMENT_NAME': 'NIRISS'}
```

functions.name: crds.core.utils.condition_source_code_keys

Convert source code expressed in terms of parkeys into source code which works
with the evalable form of the parkey. Nominally for JWST data model style keys.

```pycon
>>> condition_source_code_keys('META.INSTRUMENT.NAME != "MIRI"', ('META.INSTRUMENT.NAME',))
'META_INSTRUMENT_NAME != "MIRI"'
```

functions.name: crds.core.utils.param_combinations
documentation:
Recursively combine the value lists that appear in a keyword values dict
into dictionaries describing combinations of simple values.

More directly, it reduces a dict describing valid keyword values to
CRDS bestrefs header dicts:

{ valid keyword values dict... } -->
    [ bestrefs_header1, bestrefs_header2, ...]

or in more detail:

{ keyword1 : [values_for_keyword1, ...], ...}  -->
    [{ keyword1 : simple_value_for_keyword1, ...}, ...]

suitable for CRDS bestrefs --load-pickles,  inventing "case"

documentation:
Given an input file describing parameter values that need
to be covered,  convert the coverage specification into
discrete parameter combinations (nominally bestrefs headers)
and write them out in JSON format suitable for bestrefs.

META.INSTRUMENT.NAME:
    [ NIRCAM ]

META.EXPOSURE.READPATT:
    [DEEP8, DEEP2, MEDIUM8, MEDIUM2, SHALLOW4, SHALLOW2,
    BRIGHT2, BRIGHT1,RAPID]

Can be called from contexts where `observatory` is not known locally but
won't work correctly in all configurations of CRDS;  works best when
CRDS_OBSERVATORY is defined or `observatory` appears in CRDS_SERVER_URL.

Returns locator object named `plugin_name`.

methods.name: crds.core.utils.xcached.__call__
documentation:
Create a CachedFunction for `func` with extra qualifiers *args and **keys.
Nomnially executes at import time.

Caching decorator which supports auxilliary caching parameters.

omit_from_key lists keywords or positional indices to be excluded from cache
key creation:

```pycon
>>> @xcached(omit_from_key=[0, "x"])
... def sum(x, y, z):
...     return x + y + z
```

```pycon
>>> sum(1,2,3)
6
```

```pycon
>>> sum(2,2,3)
6
```

```pycon
>>> sum.uncached(2,2,3)
7
```

```pycon
>>> sum.readonly(2,2,3)
6
```

modules.name: crds.core.log
documentation:
Some simple console message functions w/counting features for
errors, warnings, and info. Also error exception raising and
tracebacks.

```pycon
>>> from crds.core import log
>>> log.set_test_mode()
>>> log.reset()
```

```pycon
>>> log.warning("this is a test warning.")
CRDS - WARNING - this is a test warning.
```

```pycon
>>> log.error("this is a test error.")
CRDS - ERROR - this is a test error.
```

```pycon
>>> log.error("this is another test error.")
CRDS - ERROR - this is another test error.
```

```pycon
>>> log.info("this is just informative.")
CRDS - INFO - this is just informative.
```

```pycon
>>> log.errors()
2
```

```pycon
>>> log.status()
(2, 1, 1)
```

```pycon
>>> log.standard_status()
CRDS - INFO - 2 errors
CRDS - INFO - 1 warnings
CRDS - INFO - 1 infos
```

By default verbose messages are not emitted:

```pycon
>>> log.verbose("this is a test verbose message.")
```

Calling set_verbose() turns on default verbosity=50:

```pycon
>>> old_verbose = log.set_verbose()
```

```pycon
>>> log.verbose("this is a test verbose message.")
CRDS - DEBUG - this is a test verbose message.
```

No output is expected since default verbosity=50:

```pycon
>>> log.verbose("this is a test suppressed verbose 60 message.", verbosity=60)
```

Output should now occur since verbosity=60:

```pycon
>>> log.set_verbose(60)
50
>>> log.verbose("this is a test verbose 60 message.", verbosity=60)
CRDS - DEBUG - this is a test verbose 60 message.
```

A number of context managers are defined for succinctly mapping nested
exceptions onto CRDS messages or adding information:

```pycon
>>> with log.error_on_exception("Something bad happened and we trapped it"):
...     raise ValueError("some value was bad.")
CRDS - ERROR - Something bad happened and we trapped it : some value was bad.
```

```pycon
>>> with log.augment_exception("A tad more parent info"):
...     raise ValueError("some vague deeply nested exception.")
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: A tad more parent info : some vague deeply nested exception.
```

When the exception trap mode is set to False, the exception context managers
let the exceptions pass through unmodified:

```pycon
>>> old_trap = log.set_exception_trap(False)
```

```pycon
>>> with log.error_on_exception("Something bad happened and we trapped it"):
...     raise ValueError("some value was bad.")
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: some value was bad.
```

```pycon
>>> with log.error_on_exception("Something bad happened and we trapped it"):
...     raise ValueError("some value was bad.")
...     print("this code not reached")
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: some value was bad.
```

```pycon
>>> with log.augment_exception("A tad more parent info"):
...     raise ValueError("some vague deeply nested exception.")
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: some vague deeply nested exception.
```

Command line tools control the exception trap flag using --debug-traps, this is
useful for debugging anomalies during type (or tool) development. The key points
about the exception trap flag are that (a) traps are passed through and (b) exceptions
are carefully reraised so the original traceback is not destroyed.

```pycon
>>> _ = log.set_verbose(old_verbose)
>>> _ = log.set_exception_trap(old_trap)
```

```pycon
>>> old_verbose = log.set_verbose()
```

```pycon
>>> with log.verbose_warning_on_exception("Testing expected verbose warning"):
...     raise ValueError("Force verbose warning.")
...     log.write("This code should not be executed.")
CRDS - WARNING -  Testing expected verbose warning : Force verbose warning.
```

```pycon
>>> _ = log.set_verbose(old_verbose)
```

functions.name: crds.core.log.reduced_verbosity
documentation:
Sets the global verbosity level to `reduced_level` as long as it is already below `threshhold`
within the scope of the with-block / context manager.

If the global verbosity is above `threshhold`,  don't set to `reduced_level` enabling high verbosity settings
to bypass the suppression.

modules.name: crds.core.naming
documentation:
This module contains functions related to generating and comparing the time order
of CRDS file names.   Different projects have different naming conventions.

functions.name: crds.core.naming.generate_unique_name
documentation: Given and original filepath `filename` generate a unique CRDS name for the file.

functions.name: crds.core.naming.newer
documentation:
Determine if `name1` is a more recent file than `name2` accounting for
limited differences in naming conventions. Official CDBS and CRDS names are
comparable using a simple text comparison, just not to each other.

```pycon
>>> newer("s7g1700gl_dead.fits", "hst_cos_deadtab_0001.fits")
False
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0001.fits", "s7g1700gl_dead.fits")
True
```

```pycon
>>> newer("s7g1700gl_dead.fits", "bbbbb.fits")
True
```

```pycon
>>> newer("bbbbb.fits", "s7g1700gl_dead.fits")
False
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0001.rmap", "hst_cos_deadtab_0002.rmap")
False
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0002.rmap", "hst_cos_deadtab_0001.rmap")
True
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0001.asdf", "hst_cos_deadtab_0050.fits")
True
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0051.fits", "hst_cos_deadtab_0050.asdf")
False
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0001.fits", "hst_cos_deadtab_99991.fits")
False
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_99991.fits", "hst_cos_deadtab_0001.fits")
True
```

```pycon
>>> newer("07g1700gl_dead.fits", "s7g1700gl_dead.fits")
True
```

```pycon
>>> newer("s7g1700gl_dead.fits", "07g1700gl_dead.fits")
False
```

```pycon
>>> newer("N/A", "s7g1700gl_dead.fits")
False
```

```pycon
>>> newer("07g1700gl_dead.fits", "N/A")
True
```

```pycon
>>> newer("N/A", "hst_cos_deadtab_0002.rmap")
False
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0002.rmap", "N/A")
True
```

```pycon
>>> newer("16n1832tm_tmc.fits", "06n1832tm_tmc.fits")
True
>>> newer("06n1832tm_tmc.fits", "16n1832tm_tmc.fits")
False
>>> newer("06n1832tm_tmc.fits", "16n1832tm_tmg.fits")
False
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0001.fits", "16n1832tm_tmg.fits")
CRDS - WARNING - Unhandled name comparison case:  ('crds', 'synphot')
```

```pycon
>>> newer("07g1700gl_dead.fits", "16n1832tm_tmg.fits")
CRDS - WARNING - Unhandled name comparison case:  ('newcdbs', 'synphot')
```

```pycon
>>> newer("16n1832tm_tmg.fits", "7g1700gl_dead.fits")
CRDS - WARNING - Unhandled name comparison case:  ('synphot', 'oldcdbs')
```

```pycon
>>> newer("hst_cos_deadtab_0001.fits", "17g1700gl_dead.fits")
CRDS - WARNING - Unhandled name comparison case:  ('crds', 'newcdbs')
```

```pycon
>>> newer("17g1700gl_dead.fits", "hst_cos_deadtab_0001.fits")
CRDS - WARNING - Unhandled name comparison case:  ('newcdbs', 'crds')
```

functions.name: crds.core.naming.crds_basename
documentation: basename() accounting for N/A pass thru.

functions.name: crds.core.naming.classify_name
documentation:
Classify filename `name` as "crds", "synphot", "oldcdbs", or "newcdbs".

```pycon
>>> classify_name("jwst_miri_dark_0057.fits")
'crds'
```

```pycon
>>> classify_name("s7g1700gl_dead.fits")
'oldcdbs'
```

```pycon
>>> classify_name("07g1700gl_dead.fits")
'newcdbs'
```

```pycon
>>> classify_name("16n1832tm_tmc.fits")
'newsynphot'
```

```pycon
>>> classify_name("z6n1832tm_tmc.fits")
'oldsynphot'
```

Ad hoc names are generally mistakes which are immediately replaced. Classify them
as oldcdbs (oldest) so that comparison with anything else is interpreted as a
replacement with something newer, and nominal.

```pycon
>>> classify_name("bbbbbb.fits")
'oldcdbs'
```

functions.name: crds.core.naming.crds_name
documentation:
Return True IFF `name` is a CRDS-style name, e.g. hst_acs.imap

```pycon
>>> crds_name("s7g1700gl_dead.fits")
False
>>> crds_name("1c82030ml_dead.fits")
False
>>> crds_name("hst.pmap")
True
>>> crds_name("hst_acs_darkfile_0001.fits")
True
>>> crds_name("16n1832tm_tmc.fits")
False
```

```pycon
>>> old_cdbs_name("16n1832tm_tmc.fits")

True
>>> old_cdbs_name("hst.pmap")
False
>>> old_cdbs_name("hst_acs_darkfile_0001.fits")
```

functions.name: crds.core.naming.new_cdbs_name

```pycon
>>> new_cdbs_name("1c82030ml_dead.fits")

>>> new_cdbs_name("hst_acs_darkfile_0001.fits")
```

functions.name: crds.core.naming.synphot_name

Return True IFF `name` is the name of an ETC master table file of some kind.

```pycon
>>> synphot_name("s7g1700gl_dead.fits")

>>> synphot_name("1c82030ml_dead.fits")

>>> synphot_name("16n1832tm_tmc.fits")
```

functions.name: crds.core.naming.extension_rank
documentation:
Return a date ranking for `filename` based on extension, lowest numbers are oldest.

```pycon
>>> extension_rank("fooo.r0h")
0.0
>>> extension_rank("fooo.fits")
1.0
>>> extension_rank("fooo.json")
2.0
>>> extension_rank("fooo.yaml")

>>> extension_rank("/some/path/fooo.asdf")

```

functions.name: crds.core.naming.newstyle_serial
documentation:
Return the serial number associated with a CRDS-style name.

```pycon
>>> newstyle_serial("hst_0042.pmap")

>>> newstyle_serial("hst_0990999.pmap")
990999
>>> newstyle_serial("hst_cos_0999.imap")
999
>>> newstyle_serial("hst_cos_darkfile_0998.fits")
998
>>> newstyle_serial("hst.pmap")
-1
```

modules.name: crds.core.rmap
documentation:
This module supports loading all the data components required to make
a CRDS lookup table for an instrument.

get_cached_mapping loads the closure of the given context file from the
local CRDS store, caching the result.

```pycon
>>> p = get_cached_mapping("hst.pmap")
```

The initial HST pipeline mappings are self-consistent and there are none
missing:

```pycon
>>> p.missing_mappings()
[]
```

There are 72 pmap, imap, and rmap files in the entire HST pipeline:

```pycon
>>> len(p.mapping_names()) > 50
True
```

There are over 9000 reference files known to the initial CRDS mappings scraped
from the CDBS HTML table dump:

```pycon
>>> len(p.reference_names()) > 1000
True
```

Pipeline reference files are also broken down by instrument:

```pycon
>>> sorted(p.reference_name_map().keys())
['acs', 'cos', 'nicmos', 'stis', 'wfc3', 'wfpc2']
```

```pycon
>>> i = load_mapping("hst_acs.imap")
```

The ACS instrument has 15 associated mappings, including the instrument
context:

```pycon
>>> len(i.mapping_names()) > 10
True
```

The ACS instrument has 3983 associated reference files in the hst_acs.imap
context:

```pycon
>>> len(i.reference_names()) > 2000
True
```

Active instrument references are also broken down by filetype:

```pycon
>>> sorted(i.reference_name_map()["crrejtab"])
['j4d1435lj_crr.fits', 'lci1518ej_crr.fits', 'lci1518fj_crr.fits', 'n4e12510j_crr.fits', 'n4e12511j_crr.fits']
```

```pycon
>>> r = ReferenceMapping.from_file("hst_acs_biasfile.rmap")
>>> len(r.reference_names())  > 500
True
```

functions.name: crds.core.rmap._load
documentation:
Stand-off function to call load_mapping, fetch_mapping, or get_cached_mapping
depending on the "loader" value of `keys`.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.get_cached_mapping
documentation:
Load `mapping` from the file system or cache,  adding it and all it's
descendents to the cache.

NOTE:   mutations to the mapping are reflected in the cache.   This call is
not suitable for experimental mappings which need to be reloaded from the
file system since the cached version will be returned instead.   This call
always returns the same Mapping object for a given set of parameters so it
should not be used where a copy is required.

Return a PipelineContext, InstrumentContext, or ReferenceMapping.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.fetch_mapping
documentation:
Load any `mapping`,  exploiting Mapping's already in the cache but not
adding anything extra.   This is safe for experimental mappings and temporaries
because new mappings not in the cache are not added to the cache.

This call only returns a copy of mappings not already in the cache.

Return a PipelineContext, InstrumentContext, or ReferenceMapping.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.load_mapping
documentation:
Load any `mapping`,  ignoring the cache.   Returns a unique object
for each call.   Slow but safe for any use,  reads every file and
returns a new copy.

Return a PipelineContext, InstrumentContext, or ReferenceMapping.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap._load_mapping
documentation: _load_mapping fetches `mapping` from the file system or cache.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.asmapping
documentation:
Return the Mapping object corresponding to `filename_or_mapping`.
filename_or_mapping must either be a string (filename to be loaded) or
a Mapping subclass which is simply returned.

cached can be set to:

False, "uncached"   ignore the mappings cache,  always reload,  don't add to cache
"readonly"          load the mapping from the cache if possible,  don't add to the cache if not present
True, "cached"      load the mapping from the cache if possible, add it to the cache if not present

'readonly' is for experimental/proposed mappings which should not permanently exist in the
cache because they may later be rejected and/or redefined.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.list_references
documentation: Return the list of cached references for `observatory` which match `glob_pattern`.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.list_mappings
documentation: Return the list of cached mappings for `observatory` which match `glob_pattern`.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.list_pickles
documentation: Return the list of cached mappings for `observatory` which match `glob_pattern`.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap._glob_list
documentation: Return the sorted glob of `pattern`, with/without path depending on `full_path`.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.mapping_type
documentation:
```pycon
>>> mapping_type("hst.pmap")
'pmap'
>>> mapping_type("hst_acs.imap")
'imap'
>>> mapping_type("hst_acs_biasfile.rmap")
'rmap'
>>> try:
...    mapping_type("hst_acs.foo")
... except IOError:
...    pass
>>> mapping_type(get_cached_mapping('hst.pmap'))
'pmap'
>>> mapping_type(get_cached_mapping('hst_acs.imap'))
'imap'
>>> mapping_type(get_cached_mapping('hst_acs_darkfile.rmap'))
'rmap'
```

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.is_special_value
documentation:
Return True IFF `filename` is one of the special values which
does not correspond to a file, e.g. N/A.

```pycon
>>> is_special_value("foo.fits")
False
```

```pycon
>>> is_special_value("N/A")
True
```

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.replace_rmap_text
documentation: Do simple text replacement from `old_text` to `new_text` in `rmapping`.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.get_best_references
documentation:
Compute the best references for `header` for the given CRDS
`context_file`.   This is a local computation using local rmaps and
CPU resources.   If `include` is None,  return results for all
filekinds appropriate to `header`,  otherwise return only those
filekinds listed in `include`.

functions.name: crds.core.rmap.test
documentation: Run module doctests.

classes.name: crds.core.rmap.LowerCaseDict

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.LowerCaseDict.__repr__
documentation:
>> LowerCaseDict([("this","THAT"), ("another", "(ESCAPED)")])
LowerCaseDict({'this': 'that', 'another': '(ESCAPED)'})

Used to return Mapping header string values uniformly as lower case.

```pycon
>>> d = LowerCaseDict([("this","THAT"), ("another", "(ESCAPED)")])
```

Ordinarily, all string values are mapped to lower case:

```pycon
>>> d["this"]
'that'
```

Values bracketed by () are returned unaltered in order to support header Python
expressions which are typically evaluated in the context of an incoming header
(FITS) dictionary, all upper case:

```pycon
>>> d["another"]
'(ESCAPED)'
```

classes.name: crds.core.rmap.Mapping

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.Mapping._check_type
documentation: Verify that the 'mapping' element of the header matches 'self.mapping_type'.

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.Mapping.validate_mapping
documentation:
Validate `self` only implementing any checks to be performed by
crds.certify.   ContextMappings are mostly validated at load time.
Stick extra checks for context mappings here.

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.Mapping.validate
documentation:
Perform generic recursive Mapping checks, including definition of all
required header fields as defined by Mapping class/sub-class.  Since
this requires loading all sub-mappings, it is factored out to facilitate
demand based loading elsewhere.

Other methods of validation are performed when a Mapping is loaded,  including
sha1sum and acceptability of byte codes,  neither of which requires loading
sub-mappings.

Return only those items of `header` which are required to determine

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.Mapping.difference

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.Mapping.todict
documentation:
Return a 'pure data' dictionary representation of this mapping and it's children
suitable for conversion to json.  If `recursive` is non-zero,  return that many
levels of the hierarchy starting with this one.   If recursive is zero,  only
return the filename and header of the next levels down,  not the contents.

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.Mapping.tojson

Overridden by PipelineMapping which figures it out from header.

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.Mapping.locate_file
documentation: Return the full path (in cache or absolute) of `filename` as determined by the current environment.

Mapping is the abstract baseclass for PipelineContext,
InstrumentContext, and ReferenceMapping.

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.ContextMapping.force_load
documentation: Ensure that all submappings are loaded, i.e. make artificial demand.

Add or replace and element of this mapping's selector.   For re-writing only.

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.PipelineContext.get_best_references
documentation:
Return the best references for keyword map `header`.  If `include`
is None,  collect all filekinds,  else only those listed.

methods.name: crds.core.rmap.PipelineContext.get_old_references
documentation:
Return the old references defined in keyword map `header` using this
context to define the types to return when `include` is None.

```pycon
>>> p = get_cached_mapping("hst.pmap")
```

